title: Learning diary
text:
Welcome
This is my learning diary for CASA0023: Remotely Sensing Cities and Environments.
The diary is organised by week. Each entry follows the same structure:

Summary – key ideas from the lecture/practical, in my own words

Applications – examples of how these ideas are used (papers, policy, real-world cases)

Reflection – what I understood well, what I’m unsure about, and how I might use it later



Links

🔗 GitHub Repository
📊 Slides (Week 2): Landsat Program Presentation



Module overview (quick notes)

Assessment 1 (30%): Group presentation (10 minutes), policy-focused EO pitch

Assessment 2 (70%): Weekly learning diary (this site)



How I’m working

Tools: R / RStudio, Quarto, GitHub Pages (and later Google Earth Engine)
Data note: large EO datasets are not uploaded to GitHub; only small figures used in the diary are stored here.

title: Week 1: Introduction to Remote Sensing and Spectral Analysis Practice
text:
This week’s learning made me realize that remote sensing is not simply photography, but rather the physical measurement of electromagnetic radiation (EMR) energy interactions (Brady 2021). Through the lecture, I grasped the core formula of electromagnetic waves: \(\lambda = c/v\), where wavelength is inversely proportional to frequency.






Note



Before the sensor detects the Earth’s surface, the energy undergoes complex interactions (Tempfli et al. 2009):

Atmospheric Interactions: These include Rayleigh scattering (which makes the sky blue and affects visible bands), Mie scattering (caused by aerosols), and non-selective scattering (caused by clouds). This explains why optical remote sensing faces massive atmospheric correction challenges in cloudy Guiyang.
Surface Interactions: Energy is absorbed, transmitted, or reflected by the surface. The sensor’s task is precisely to capture this reflected energy, which carries information about the surface features.






The lecture emphasized the “four resolutions” that determine the success of remote sensing applications:

Spatial Resolution: Determines the smallest observable feature. Sentinel-2’s 10m resolution offers a significant advantage at the urban scale (Jensen 2015).
Spectral Resolution: The number and width of bands. I realized that a “spectral signature” is the physical fingerprint of a surface feature, allowing us to distinguish extremely similar materials through their responses across different bands.
Temporal Resolution: The revisit period, which is crucial for dynamic environmental monitoring.
Radiometric Resolution: The sensor’s sensitivity, or quantization level (e.g., 12-bit provides 4,096 brightness levels).




I selected my hometown, Guiyang, as the study area. Beyond my emotional connection, Guiyang holds significant research value geographically:

Complexity of Karst Topography: Guiyang’s rugged terrain leads to a pronounced “Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function (BRDF)” effect, meaning observation and illumination angles significantly alter the apparent reflectance of surface features.
Data Balancing Act: While Sentinel-2 excels in spatial precision, Landsat’s historical data archive remains irreplaceable for tracking the urban expansion trajectory of Guiyang over the past few decades that I recall from my childhood (Tempfli et al. 2009).




Figure 1: Pre-processing workflow in SNAP, including resampling and masking Sentinel-2 data for Guiyang.

title: Week 1
text:
Summary (what I did & key ideas)

Key ideas from Lecture 1

Remote sensing measures electromagnetic radiation (EMR) interacting with Earth surface/atmosphere.
EMR described by wavelength and frequency:
\[\lambda = \frac{c}{v}\]
Different materials have different spectral responses → band combos/indices work.
Observations vary with illumination/viewing geometry (BRDF) → comparisons need consistent preprocessing.



Workflow (what I did in order)

Selected city + searched scenes by date & cloud cover.
Downloaded Sentinel-2 L2A (tile-based) and Landsat L2 (bundle).
Explored bands/composites in QGIS/SNAP.
Checked image statistics (histogram/scatterplot).
(Optional) Exported subsets for later analysis in R.



Practical 1 notes

Sentinel-2 (Copernicus Browser): (fill steps + settings)
QGIS exploration: (bands, stack, composites, contrast enhancement observation)
SNAP exploration: (open zip/MTL, RGB, histogram, scatterplot)
Resampling / masking decisions (if done): (NN/bilinear? 10m→20m? why)

title: Week 2: 传感器展示 (Sensor Presentation)
section: 2. 传感器总结与独立思考 (Summary)
text:
2. 传感器总结与独立思考 (Summary)
虽然幻灯片涵盖了 Landsat 的基础知识，但在我的独立探索中，我将其与另一款主流光学传感器 Sentinel-2 进行了横向对比。
尽管 Sentinel-2 提供了更高的空间分辨率（最高 10米）和更快的重访周期（5天），但 Landsat 在城市气候研究中仍然具有不可替代的地位。这主要是因为它搭载了热红外传感器 (TIRS) 以及拥有无与伦比的历史数据存档。
表1：城市遥感应用快速对比









核心特征
Landsat 8/9 (OLI & TIRS)
Sentinel-2 (MSI)
对城市研究的启示




光学分辨率
30米
10米 - 60米
Sentinel-2 更适合绘制精细的街道级植被图。


热红外波段
有 (原始 100米，重采样至 30米)
无
Landsat 是绘制城市热岛效应 (UHI) 不可或缺的工具。


数据存档
自 1972 年起
自 2015 年起
Landsat 是追踪几十年跨度城市扩张的唯一选择。
crumbs: 3  Week 2: 传感器展示 (Sensor Presentation)

title: Week 2: 传感器展示 (Sensor Presentation)
text:
3. 应用案例与文献 (Applications)
多光谱和热红外数据的结合，使 Landsat 成为城市环境监测的得力工具。一个经典的应用是计算归一化植被指数 (NDVI) 来评估城市绿色基础设施，进而将其与地表温度 (LST) 的变化进行相关性分析。
这种关系通常使用以下公式进行量化计算：
\[NDVI = \frac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}\]
对应到 Landsat 8/9 的具体波段，公式转换为：
\[NDVI = \frac{Band 5 - Band 4}{Band 5 + Band 4}\] ### 案例研究：城市热岛效应 (UHI)
利用 Landsat 8/9 的热红外波段 (TIRS)，我们可以反演地表温度 (LST)。下方展示了典型的城市热力分布，其中亮红色代表由于密集的建筑和道路引起的高温区域。



Landsat 衍生出的地表温度 (LST) 展示图。红色区域对应城市热岛。



分析提示：这类热力图是城市规划中评估绿色基础设施（如公园、屋顶绿化）缓解温度效果的关键依据。

title: Week 2: 传感器展示 (Sensor Presentation)
text:
4. 学习反思 (Reflection)

关于传感器与对地观测
本周我最大的收获是认识到开放数据政策 (Open Data Policies) 的至关重要性。Wulder 等人 (2012) 准确地指出，开放 Landsat 存档彻底改变了整个遥感科学的发展轨迹。然而，我也意识到了遥感技术中的“权衡 (trade-offs)”：你无法拥有一切。Landsat 牺牲了部分空间分辨率（30米）来换取更广阔的覆盖范围和热红外探测能力。在未来的工作中，我对数据融合 (Data Fusion) 非常感兴趣——即将 Landsat 的热红外数据与 Sentinel-2 的高分辨率光学数据结合，从而在精确的城市微气候分析中实现“两全其美”。


关于技术流程 (可重复性研究)
除了了解传感器本身，制作这份幻灯片也是一段陡峭但回报丰厚的学习曲线。从传统的 PowerPoint 制作方式过渡到代码驱动的演示文稿 (Xaringan)，并通过 GitHub Pages 将其发布到网上，迫使我深入理解了可重复性 (Reproducibility) 的概念。使用纯文本 (Markdown) 编写幻灯片并直接集成 R 代码，意味着我的数据分析和结果展示不再是脱节的，这对我未来的学术研究大有裨益。
crumbs: 3  Week 2: 传感器展示 (Sensor Presentation)

Loveland, T. R., & Dwyer, J. L. (2012). Landsat: Building a strong future, Remote Sensing of Environment, 122, pp. 22–29. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2011.09.022

title: Week 2: Sensor Presentation
text:
2. Sensor Summary and Independent Reflection
Although the slides cover the fundamental knowledge of Landsat, in my independent exploration I conducted a horizontal comparison with another mainstream optical sensor, Sentinel-2.
Although Sentinel-2 provides higher spatial resolution (up to 10 meters) and a faster revisit cycle (5 days), Landsat still holds an irreplaceable position in urban climate research. This is mainly because it carries a Thermal Infrared Sensor (TIRS) and possesses an unparalleled historical data archive.
Table 1: Quick Comparison for Urban Remote Sensing Applications









Key Feature
Landsat 8/9 (OLI & TIRS)
Sentinel-2 (MSI)
Implications for Urban Research




Optical Resolution
30 m
10 m – 60 m
Sentinel-2 is more suitable for mapping fine-scale street-level vegetation.


Thermal Infrared Bands
Yes (originally 100 m, resampled to 30 m)
No
Landsat is indispensable for mapping Urban Heat Island (UHI) effects.


Data Archive
Since 1972
Since 2015
Landsat is the only option for tracking urban expansion over decades.

title: Week 2: Sensor Presentation
text:
3. Applications and Literature
The combination of multispectral and thermal infrared data makes Landsat a powerful tool for urban environmental monitoring. A classic application is calculating the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) to assess urban green infrastructure, and then analyzing its correlation with Land Surface Temperature (LST).
This relationship is typically quantified using the following formula:
\[NDVI = \frac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}\]
For Landsat 8/9, the formula corresponds to specific bands as:
\[NDVI = \frac{Band\ 5 - Band\ 4}{Band\ 5 + Band\ 4}\]

Case Study: Urban Heat Island (UHI)
Using the thermal infrared bands (TIRS) of Landsat 8/9, we can retrieve Land Surface Temperature (LST). The figure below shows a typical urban thermal distribution, where bright red areas represent high-temperature zones caused by dense buildings and roads.



Land Surface Temperature (LST) derived from Landsat. Red areas indicate urban heat islands.



Analysis Tip: Such thermal maps are key evidence in urban planning when evaluating the cooling effects of green infrastructure (e.g., parks and green roofs).

title: Week 2: Sensor Presentation
text:
4. Learning Reflection

On Sensors and Earth Observation
My biggest takeaway this week is recognizing the crucial importance of Open Data Policies. Wulder et al. (2012) accurately pointed out that opening the Landsat archive fundamentally transformed the trajectory of remote sensing science. However, I also became aware of the “trade-offs” in remote sensing technology: you cannot have everything. Landsat sacrifices some spatial resolution (30 m) in exchange for broader coverage and thermal infrared capability. In future work, I am particularly interested in Data Fusion—combining Landsat’s thermal infrared data with Sentinel-2’s high-resolution optical data to achieve the “best of both worlds” in precise urban microclimate analysis.


On Technical Workflow (Reproducible Research)
Beyond understanding the sensor itself, producing this slide deck was a steep but rewarding learning curve. Transitioning from traditional PowerPoint-based production to code-driven presentations (Xaringan), and publishing them online via GitHub Pages, forced me to deeply understand the concept of Reproducibility. Writing slides in plain text (Markdown) and directly integrating R code means that my data analysis and presentation of results are no longer disconnected—an approach that will greatly benefit my future academic research.

title: Week 2: Sensor Presentation
text:
5. References


Kirenz, Jan K. 2023. “Build Presentations in R.” 2023. https://www.kirenz.com/slides/xaringan-demo-slides.html#1.


Quarto. 2022. “Quarto - Get Started.” https://quarto.org/docs/books/#quick-start.


Xie, Yihui. 2022. “With Quarto Coming, Is R Markdown Going Away? No.” April 2022. https://yihui.org/rmarkdown/xaringan.


Xie, Yihui, J. J. Allaire, and Garrett Grolemund. 2022. R Markdown: The Definitive Guide. Chapman; Hall/CRC. https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/.

title: 第一周：遥感导论与光谱分析实战
text:
1.1 传感器特性：Sentinel-2 MSI
本周的学习重点在于理解对地观测传感器的核心参数。在实践中，我处理了覆盖中国贵阳市的 Sentinel-2 MSI (Level-2A) 影像。该产品提供地表反射率数据，对于精确的光谱分析至关重要。Sentinel-2 凭借其平衡的“四种分辨率”在城市研究中表现卓越：

空间分辨率：核心波段达到 10m，能够捕捉精细的城市纹理。
光谱分辨率：包含 13 个光谱波段，涵盖从可见光到短波红外（SWIR）。
时间分辨率：凭借 A/B 双星实现 5 天的重访周期。
辐射分辨率：12 位深度，能够存储 4,096 个亮度等级。




SNAP 中的重采样与裁剪处理过程，展示了针对贵阳行政边界的初步处理。




1.2 研究区域选择：为什么选择贵阳？
贵阳不仅是我的家乡，也是研究中国西南山地城市化的典型案例。

情感联结：作为在这座城市长大的人，通过卫星之眼重新审视家乡的变迁，赋予了这项科研任务深层的人文关怀。
研究价值：贵阳独特的喀斯特地貌和多云气候给光学遥感带来了巨大挑战。
分辨率重要性：贵阳剧烈的地形起伏和快速的城市扩张，使得 Sentinel-2 的 10m 分辨率在识别碎片化绿地和高密度建筑群时显得尤为重要。



1.3 独立思考：分辨率的权衡与数据丰裕
在对比中我发现，Sentinel-2 的高时间分辨率非常适合监测贵阳多云天气下的影像缝隙。然而，Landsat 自 1972 年以来的存档数据对于追踪我记忆中家乡几十年的变迁是不可替代的。这种“时间跨度”与“空间精度”的权衡，说明了多源数据融合才是未来城市研究的必然趋势。

title: 第一周：遥感导论与光谱分析实战
section: 2. 应用案例：地物增强与全球制图 (Applications)
text:
2. 应用案例：地物增强与全球制图 (Applications)

2.1 缨帽变换 (Tasseled Cap Transformation, TCT)
在应用层面，我不仅观察了原始波段，还在 SNAP 中执行了缨帽变换。通过波段运算，我得到了三个具有明确物理意义的分量：

亮度 (Brightness)：反映了家乡城市建设用地的反射强度。
绿度 (Greenness)：突出了贵阳作为山水城市丰富的植被信号。
湿度 (Wetness)：有效识别了南明河及红枫湖等关键水体。




SNAP 中的 TCT RGB 显示效果图（R:亮度, G:绿度, B:湿度），绿色代表森林，亮粉色代表城市建筑。


通过生成散点图 (Scatter Plot)，我观察到地物在“亮度-绿度”空间中呈现出经典的“缨帽”形状分布。


2.2 全球尺度应用：ESA WorldCover
Sentinel-2 数据被广泛应用于 ESA WorldCover 10m 等项目。这种高精度地图允许城市规划者从社区尺度区分建筑区与绿色基础设施，这对我分析贵阳的城市绿地可达性具有重要启发。

title: 第一周：遥感导论与光谱分析实战
section: 3. 技术流程：从 SNAP 到 R 的光谱统计分析 (Work Logic)
text:
3. 技术流程：从 SNAP 到 R 的光谱统计分析 (Work Logic)

3.1 空间对齐与 POI 采样
在 QGIS 中，我验证了 Sentinel 和 Landsat 影像的地理配准精度。通过 同步视图 (Synchronise Views) 功能，我在家乡熟悉的坐标上精确标注了水体、森林、城市等兴趣点 (POIs) 样本。



QGIS 验证截图：展示了多源影像图层与数字化 POI 矢量文件的完美空间套合。




3.2 基于 R 的光谱签名分析
在 R 中，我使用 terra 包提取了像元值，并计算了不同地物的平均光谱特征：

光谱曲线绘制：我计算了各波段下的平均反射率。正如实验所示，光谱特征代表了地物在不同波长下的“指纹”。
密度图分析：通过密度图观察到城市像元反射率的高异质性，反映了贵阳复杂的建筑材质分布。




在 R 中生成的贵阳典型地物光谱签名曲线（含标准差误差棒）。
crumbs: 2  第一周：遥感导论与光谱分析实战

title: 第一周：遥感导论与光谱分析实战
text:
4. 学习反思 (Reflection)

4.1 统计学差异的物理本质
在实验中，我对贵阳城市样本的 Sentinel 和 Landsat 数据进行了 Welch t 检验。结果如下： * 统计量：\(t = -214.78\)。 * 显著性：\(p < 2.2e-16\)。
这种极显著差异提醒我，遥感中的“不一致”并非单纯的误差，而是源于两者的光谱响应函数以及大气校正算法（BOA）的不同。在跨传感器分析中，执行传感器定标是确保数据一致性的关键步骤。


4.2 技术成长与未来展望
通过本周的学习，我不仅学会了封装 band_fun 函数来实现自动化提取，更学会了如何用数据去讲述家乡的故事。 * 技能提升：掌握了从 SNAP 空间处理到 R 语言统计的可重复性工作流。 * 未来目标：我计划探索如何利用机器学习算法，针对贵阳这种复杂地形进行更深度的土地覆盖变化检测。
crumbs: 2  第一周：遥感导论与光谱分析实战

title: 第 1 周：遥感科学导论与光谱分析实战
text:
5. 参考文献 (References)

Drusch, M., Del Bello, U., Carlier, S., Colin, O., Fernandez, V., Gascon, F., Hoersch, B., Isola, C., Laberinti, P., Martimort, P., & Meygret, A. (2012). Sentinel-2: ESA’s Optical High-Resolution Mission for GMES Operational Services, Remote Sensing of Environment, 120, pp. 25-36.
Wulder, M. A., Masek, J. G., Cohen, W. B., Loveland, T. R., & Woodcock, C. E. (2012). Opening the archive: How free data has enabled the science and monitoring promise of Landsat, Remote Sensing of Environment, 122, pp. 2-10.
Zanaga, D., Van De Kerchove, R., De Keersmaecker, W., Souverijns, N., Brockmann, C., Quast, R., Wevers, J., Grosu, A., Paccini, A., Vergnaud, S., & Cartus, O. (2021). ESA WorldCover 10 m 2020 v100, Zenodo.
crumbs: 2  第 1 周：遥感科学导论与光谱分析实战

title: 第 1 周：遥感科学导论与光谱分析实战
text:
1.1 遥感物理基础与电磁辐射 (EMR)
本周的学习使我认识到遥感并非简单的摄影，而是对电磁辐射能量交互的物理测量。通过讲义，我掌握了电磁波的核心公式：\(\lambda = c/v\)，其中波长与频率成反比。
在传感器探测地表之前，能量经历了复杂的交互过程：

大气交互：包括瑞利散射（导致天空变蓝且影响可见光波段）、米氏散射（气溶胶引起）和非选择性散射（云层引起）。这解释了为什么在多云的贵阳，光学遥感面临着巨大的大气校正挑战。
地表交互：能量被地表吸收、透射或反射。传感器的任务正是捕捉这部分携带地物信息的反射能量。



1.2 四种分辨率的批判性审视
讲义强调了决定遥感应用成败的“四种分辨率”：

空间分辨率：决定了能看到的最小地物，Sentinel-2 的 10m 分辨率在城市尺度上具有显著优势。
光谱分辨率：波段的数量和宽度。我意识到“光谱签名”是地物的物理指纹，通过不同波段的响应可以区分极其相似的材料。
时间分辨率：重访周期。在动态监测中至关重要。
辐射分辨率：传感器的敏感度，即量化等级（如 12-bit 提供 4096 个亮度级）。



1.3 独立思考：为什么选择家乡贵阳？
我选择了我的家乡贵阳作为研究区。贵阳不仅具有情感层面的连接，在地理学上也极具研究价值：

喀斯特地形复杂性：贵阳崎岖的地形导致了显著的“双向反射分布函数 (BRDF)”效应，即观测角度和光照角度会显著改变地物的表观反射率。
数据权衡 (Balancing Act)：Sentinel-2 虽然在空间精度上表现卓越，但对于追踪我童年记忆中贵阳过去几十年的扩张轨迹，Landsat 的历史存档数据是不可替代的选择。




图 1：在 SNAP 中对贵阳市 Sentinel-2 数据进行重采样与掩膜裁剪的预处理流程。
crumbs: 2  第 1 周：遥感科学导论与光谱分析实战

title: 第 1 周：遥感科学导论与光谱分析实战
text:
2. 内容应用与案例分析 (Applications)

2.1 缨帽变换 (TCT) 在城市形态学中的应用
在实践中，我应用了 缨帽变换 (Tasseled Cap Transformation)。这不仅仅是一种降维技术，更是一种生物物理指标的提取：

亮度 (Brightness)：有效提取了贵阳近年来兴起的“大数据中心”等高密度建筑区。
绿度 (Greenness)：帮助我识别了贵阳作为国家森林城市在快速扩张中保留的绿色廊道。
湿度 (Wetness)：成功提取了南明河与红枫湖水系，这对于评价城市蓝绿空间的生态功能至关重要。




图 2：贵阳市 TCT RGB 渲染图（R:亮度, G:绿度, B:湿度）。亮色区域清晰展示了城市中心的建筑密度。




2.2 跨传感器研究：ESA WorldCover 的启示
讲义中提到的 ESA WorldCover 10m 项目展示了 10m 分辨率在实时城市监测中的巨大潜力。与传统的 30m Landsat 数据相比，高分辨率 Sentinel 数据能显著减少城市边缘的“混合像元”现象。然而我也认识到，在多云的高原地区，单一的光学应用存在瓶颈，未来必须结合能“穿透云层”的 SAR 主动传感器数据。



3.1 从 SNAP 到 R 的自动化工作流
我的技术路径实现了从空间处理到统计建模的闭环：

SNAP 处理：完成了重采样、地理配准以及基于矢量边界的影像裁剪。
R 语言采样：利用 terra 包提取像元值，并封装了 band_fun 函数实现批量提取和长格式 (Long Format) 整理。
QGIS 验证：确保了多源影像在相同地理空间下的完美套合。







图 3-A：Landsat-9 影像与 POI 样点的空间套合







图 3-B：Sentinel-2 影像与 POI 样点的空间套合





图 3：QGIS 空间一致性验证。多源卫星影像与我数字化的训练样点（POIs）在同一坐标系统下完美对齐，这是保证后续 R 语言像元提取准确性的核心前提。


3.2 基于 R 的光谱签名统计分析
利用 terra::extract 和 tidyverse 的长格式转换，我提取了贵阳市典型地物的光谱响应值，并绘制了特征曲线（图 4）：

植被的红边效应 (Red Edge)：在光谱曲线 (Plot A) 中，森林样本（红线）在可见光波段（Band 1-3）反射率较低，但在近红外波段（Band 4）出现急剧攀升。这完美印证了健康植被叶肉细胞强烈反射 NIR 能量的生物物理特性。
水体的吸收特性：水体样本（蓝线）在所有波段均保持极低反射率，且在 NIR 波段呈现下降趋势，证实了水体对长波能量的强烈吸收。
城市地表的极高异质性 (High Heterogeneity)：这是最显著的发现。光谱曲线显示城市像元（绿线）在各波段具有极大的标准差（误差棒）；同时，密度分布图 (Plot B) 显示城市像元的反射率分布极其宽泛平缓。这在统计学上反映了贵阳市中心由混凝土、沥青、金属屋顶和碎片化绿地组成的极其复杂的混合地表形态。




图 4：在 R 中生成的贵阳典型地物光谱签名曲线（含标准差误差棒）。
crumbs: 2  第 1 周：遥感科学导论与光谱分析实战

title: 第 1 周：遥感科学导论与光谱分析实战
section: 3. 技术流程与实践逻辑 (Work Logic)
text:
3. 技术流程与实践逻辑 (Work Logic)

3.1 从 SNAP 到 R 的自动化工作流
我的技术路径实现了从空间处理到统计建模的闭环： 1. SNAP 处理：完成了重采样、地理配准以及基于矢量边界的影像裁剪。 2. R 语言采样：利用 terra 包提取像元值，并封装了 band_fun 函数实现批量提取和长格式 (Long Format) 整理。 3. QGIS 验证：确保了多源影像在相同地理空间下的完美套合。



图 3：QGIS 验证：展示了 Sentinel 和 Landsat 影像在贵阳城区 POI 样点上的高度空间一致性。




3.2 基于 R 的光谱签名统计分析
利用 terra::extract 和 tidyverse 的长格式转换，我提取了贵阳市典型地物的光谱响应值，并绘制了特征曲线（图 4）：

植被的红边效应 (Red Edge)：在光谱曲线 (Plot A) 中，森林样本（红线）在可见光波段（Band 1-3）反射率较低，但在近红外波段（Band 4）出现急剧攀升。这完美印证了健康植被叶肉细胞强烈反射 NIR 能量的生物物理特性。
水体的吸收特性：水体样本（蓝线）在所有波段均保持极低反射率，且在 NIR 波段呈现下降趋势，证实了水体对长波能量的强烈吸收。
城市地表的极高异质性 (High Heterogeneity)：这是最显著的发现。光谱曲线显示城市像元（绿线）在各波段具有极大的标准差（误差棒）；同时，密度分布图 (Plot B) 显示城市像元的反射率分布极其宽泛平缓。这在统计学上反映了贵阳市中心由混凝土、沥青、金属屋顶和碎片化绿地组成的极其复杂的混合地表形态。




图 4：在 R 中生成的贵阳典型地物光谱签名曲线（含标准差误差棒）。
crumbs: 2  第 1 周：遥感科学导论与光谱分析实战

title: 第 1 周：遥感科学导论与光谱分析实战
text:
4. 个人反思 (Personal Reflection)

4.1 统计学显著性 vs. 遥感物理意义
在本周的实验中，我对 Sentinel 和 Landsat 在家乡城市区域的像元值进行了 Welch t 检验。



图 5：在 RStudio 中运行的 Welch t 检验结果，显示两类传感器的像元值具有极显著的统计学差异。



统计结果：如图 5 所示，\(t = -214.78\)，\(p < 2.2 \times 10^{-16}\)，具有极显著差异。
深度反思：作为一名研究者，我深刻理解到“显著差异”并不代表数据处理存在错误，而是客观反映了两者的物理差异——包括光谱响应函数、大气校正算法（如 Sentinel 的 Sen2Cor 与 Landsat 的 LaSRC）以及各自的辐射定标系数的不同。这提醒我，在未来的城市气候研究中，跨传感器的数据融合不能简单粗暴地拼接，必须预先进行精密的传感器间交叉定标 (Cross-calibration)。



4.2 技能成长与未来愿景
通过这一周的学习，我不仅掌握了操作工具（SNAP, R, QGIS），更重要的是学会了可重复性研究 (Reproducibility) 的代码思维。未来，我计划进一步探索基于提取的光谱特征进行机器学习分类，用客观的数据量化我家乡过去十年城市化对生态本底的真实影响。
crumbs: 2  第 1 周：遥感科学导论与光谱分析实战

title: Week 1: Introduction to Remote Sensing and Spectral Analysis Practice
text:
This week’s learning made me realize that remote sensing is not simply photography, but rather the physical measurement of electromagnetic radiation (EMR) energy interactions ((Brady 2021)). Through the lecture, I grasped the core formula of electromagnetic waves: \(\lambda = c/v\), where wavelength is inversely proportional to frequency.






Note



Before the sensor detects the Earth’s surface, the energy undergoes complex interactions ((Tempfli et al. 2009)):

Atmospheric Interactions: These include Rayleigh scattering (which makes the sky blue and affects visible bands), Mie scattering (caused by aerosols), and non-selective scattering (caused by clouds). This explains why optical remote sensing faces massive atmospheric correction challenges in cloudy Guiyang.
Surface Interactions: Energy is absorbed, transmitted, or reflected by the surface. The sensor’s task is precisely to capture this reflected energy, which carries information about the surface features.






The lecture emphasized the “four resolutions” that determine the success of remote sensing applications:

Spatial Resolution: Determines the smallest observable feature. Sentinel-2’s 10m resolution offers a significant advantage at the urban scale ((Jensen 2015)).
Spectral Resolution: The number and width of bands. I realized that a “spectral signature” is the physical fingerprint of a surface feature, allowing us to distinguish extremely similar materials through their responses across different bands.
Temporal Resolution: The revisit period, which is crucial for dynamic environmental monitoring.
Radiometric Resolution: The sensor’s sensitivity, or quantization level (e.g., 12-bit provides 4,096 brightness levels).




I selected my hometown, Guiyang, as the study area. Beyond my emotional connection, Guiyang holds significant research value geographically:

Complexity of Karst Topography: Guiyang’s rugged terrain leads to a pronounced “Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function (BRDF)” effect, meaning observation and illumination angles significantly alter the apparent reflectance of surface features.
Data Balancing Act: While Sentinel-2 excels in spatial precision, Landsat’s historical data archive remains irreplaceable for tracking the urban expansion trajectory of Guiyang over the past few decades that I recall from my childhood ((Tempfli et al. 2009)).




Figure 1: Pre-processing workflow in SNAP, including resampling and masking Sentinel-2 data for Guiyang.

title: Week 1: Introduction to Remote Sensing and Spectral Analysis Practice
text:
2. Applications

2.1 Tasseled Cap Transformation (TCT) in Urban Morphology
In practice, I applied the Tasseled Cap Transformation (TCT). This is not just a dimensionality reduction technique, but an extraction of biophysical indicators:

Brightness: Effectively extracted the high-density built-up areas, such as the newly emerged “Big Data Centers” in Guiyang.
Greenness: Helped me identify the green corridors preserved during the rapid expansion of Guiyang, a designated National Forest City.
Wetness: Successfully extracted the Nanming River and Hongfeng Lake water systems, which is vital for evaluating the ecological functions of urban blue-green spaces.



2.2 Cross-Sensor Research: Insights from ESA WorldCover
The ESA WorldCover 10m project mentioned in the lecture demonstrates the tremendous potential of 10m resolution in real-time urban monitoring. Compared to traditional 30m Landsat data, high-resolution Sentinel data can significantly reduce “mixed pixels” at urban edges (Jensen 2015). However, I also recognize that in cloudy, high-altitude regions, single optical applications have bottlenecks. In the future, it is essential to integrate active SAR sensor data capable of “penetrating clouds.”

title: Week 1: Introduction to Remote Sensing and Spectral Analysis Practice
text:
3. Technical Workflow and Statistical Analysis

3.1 Automated Workflow from SNAP to R
My technical pathway achieved a closed loop from spatial processing to statistical modeling:

SNAP Processing: Completed resampling, georegistration, and image subsetting based on vector boundaries.
R Sampling: Utilized the terra package to extract pixel values and encapsulated a band_fun function to achieve batch extraction and Long Format data tidying.
QGIS Validation: Ensured perfect spatial alignment of multi-source imagery within the same geographical space.







Figure 3-A: Spatial alignment of Landsat-9 imagery and POIs







Figure 3-B: Spatial alignment of Sentinel-2 imagery and POIs





Figure 3: QGIS spatial consistency validation. The perfect alignment of multi-source satellite imagery and my digitized training points (POIs) in the same coordinate system is the core prerequisite ensuring the accuracy of subsequent pixel extractions in R.


3.2 R-Based Spectral Signature Statistical Analysis
Using terra::extract and long-format conversion via tidyverse, I extracted the spectral response values of typical land covers in Guiyang and plotted their signature curves (Figure 4):






Important




The Red Edge Effect of Vegetation: In the spectral curve (Plot A), the forest sample (red line) exhibits low reflectance in the visible bands (Bands 1-3) but a sharp surge in the near-infrared band (Band 4). This perfectly corroborates the biophysical characteristic of healthy mesophyll cells strongly reflecting NIR energy.
Absorption Characteristics of Water: The water sample (blue line) maintains extremely low reflectance across all bands and shows a downward trend in the NIR band, confirming the strong absorption of long-wave energy by water.
High Heterogeneity of Urban Surfaces: This is the most striking finding. The spectral curve shows that urban pixels (green line) possess massive standard deviations (error bars) across all bands; meanwhile, the density distribution plot (Plot B) reveals an extremely broad and flat reflectance distribution for urban pixels. Statistically, this reflects the highly complex and mixed surface morphology of downtown Guiyang, which comprises concrete, asphalt, metal roofs, and fragmented green spaces.






Figure 4: Spectral signature curves of typical land covers in Guiyang generated in R (with standard deviation error bars).

title: Week 1: Introduction to Remote Sensing and Spectral Analysis Practice
text:
4. Personal Reflection

4.1 Statistical Significance vs. Physical Meaning
In this week’s practical, I performed a Welch t-test on the pixel values of Sentinel-2 and Landsat-9 within the urban areas of my hometown, Guiyang.



Figure 5: Welch t-test results running in RStudio, demonstrating a highly significant statistical difference in pixel values between the two sensors.



Statistical Results: As shown in Figure 5, \(t = -214.78\), \(p < 2.2 \times 10^{-16}\), indicating a highly significant difference.
Deep Reflection: As a researcher, I deeply understand that this “significant difference” does not imply a data processing error; rather, it objectively reflects the inherent physical divergence between the two sensor systems.


💡 Physical Mechanism Analysis:

Spectral Response Functions (SRF): Each sensor “samples” the electromagnetic spectrum through slightly different windows. Even for the “same” Near-Infrared (NIR) band, the bandwidth and center wavelength differ, leading to varying sensitivity to Guiyang’s lush subtropical vegetation (Butcher 2016).
Atmospheric Correction Divergence: The complex aerosol conditions in Guiyang’s mountainous Karst landscape pose challenges for algorithms like Sen2Cor (Sentinel) and LaSRC (Landsat). These subtle differences in atmospheric path radiance removal are amplified in statistical tests.
Geographic Context: Guiyang’s rugged terrain introduces bidirectional reflectance distribution function (BRDF) effects. The different overpass times and viewing angles of the two satellites mean they capture the “same” surface under different illumination geometries, which is statistically significant but physically expected.

Conclusion: This reminds me that in future urban climate studies, cross-sensor data fusion cannot simply be pieced together abruptly. Precise cross-calibration or the use of Harmonized Landsat Sentinel (HLS) products is a prerequisite for robust longitudinal analysis (Jensen 2015).



4.2 Skill Growth and Future Vision


From Tool-User to Critical Analyst
Through this week’s learning, I have not only mastered operational tools (SNAP, R, QGIS) but, more importantly, acquired a coding mindset for Reproducible Research.

Methodological Shift: Moving beyond “point-and-click” software GUI has allowed me to automate the spectral signature extraction process. Using R ensures that my analysis of Guiyang’s urbanization is transparent and repeatable by others—a core tenet of modern geographic data science.
Overcoming the “Cloud” Bottleneck: While optical data provided a strong start, the high cloud frequency in Guiyang remains a limitation. Moving forward, I plan to further explore machine learning classification based on these extracted spectral features, but more importantly, I look forward to integrating SAR (Synthetic Aperture Radar) data in later weeks to “see through” the clouds.
Future Vision: My ultimate goal is to use this methodological prototype to quantify the true impact of rapid urban expansion on the ecological baseline of my hometown over the past decade, providing objective evidence for sustainable urban planning in Karst regions.

title: Week 1: Introduction to Remote Sensing and Spectral Analysis Practice
text:
5. References


Brady, M. 2021. “Remote Sensing for Dummies.” https://storymaps.arcgis.com/stories/cb1577b0f5bc485c974b4ea19d52282d.


Butcher, Ginger. 2016. Tour of the Electromagnetic Spectrum. Third. National Aeronautics; Space Administration, Washington, DC. https://science.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/tour-of-the-ems-tagged-v7-0.pdf.


Jensen, John R. 2015. Introductory Digital Image Processing: A Remote Sensing Perspective. Prentice-Hall Inc. https://read.kortext.com/reader/pdf/1872407/Cover.


Tempfli, K, Gc Huurneman, Wh Bakker, L. L. Janssen, W Feringa, A Gieske, K Grabmaier, et al. 2009. Principles of Remote Sensing: An Introductory Textbook. International Institute for Geo-Information Science; Earth Observation. https://webapps.itc.utwente.nl/librarywww/papers_2009/general/principlesremotesensing.pdf.

title: Week 2: Sensor Presentation
text:
2. Content Summary

2.1 Horizontal Comparison with Sentinel-2

Spatial Resolution: Sentinel-2 provides finer spatial resolution in some key optical bands, while Landsat 8/9 offers stronger long-term consistency at 30 m.
Spectral Advantage: A key strength of Landsat is that it carries both optical and thermal sensors, which makes it especially useful for urban thermal studies.



2.2 Satellite Payloads and Instrumentation

OLI (Operational Land Imager): Captures data in the visible, near-infrared, and shortwave infrared parts of the spectrum.
TIRS (Thermal Infrared Sensor): Records thermal information that can be used in land surface temperature analysis.




Figure 1: Schematic of the Landsat 8-9 satellite platform highlighting the OLI and TIRS sensor payloads. Source: NASA.




2.3 Urban Research Significance

LST Retrieval: Estimate Land Surface Temperature (LST) from Landsat thermal data, most commonly using Band 10.
UHI Analysis: Identify local temperature differences to map Urban Heat Islands (UHI).
Climate Policy: Use these data to support urban planning and help address heat-related risks in dense city areas.







Why Landsat for Urban Studies?



An important advantage of Landsat is its thermal bands, which make it possible to estimate Land Surface Temperature (LST). This is particularly useful for studying the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect.





Figure 2: Spectral band comparison between Landsat 8/9 OLI/TIRS and Sentinel-2 MSI sensors.











Key Feature
Landsat 8/9 (OLI & TIRS)
Sentinel-2 (MSI)
Implications for Urban Research




Optical Resolution
30 m
10 m – 60 m
Sentinel-2 is more suitable for detailed vegetation mapping at street level.


Thermal Infrared
Yes
No
Landsat is more suitable for heat mapping because it includes thermal bands.


Data Archive
Since 1972
Since 2015
Landsat is one of the most important sources for tracking long-term urban expansion.

title: Week 2: Sensor Presentation
text:
3. Applications

3.1 Environmental Indices and Monitoring

Vegetation Analysis: Calculate NDVI to measure the presence and condition of urban green spaces.
Cooling Effects: Use spectral data to examine how green infrastructure may help reduce urban temperatures.

\[NDVI = \frac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}\]


3.2 Spectral Signature Integrity

Data Acquisition: Select suitable multispectral bands (Red and NIR) for the study area.
Signature Analysis: Distinguish land covers such as asphalt and vegetation by comparing their reflectance curves.
Validation: Compare the results with ground-truth data or high-resolution imagery to improve mapping reliability.







Spectral Signature Analysis



Distinguishing between land covers requires the comparison of spectral signatures across multiple bands, which helps improve both interpretation and mapping quality.





Figure 3: Spectral signature curves of typical land covers in Guiyang generated in R (with standard deviation error bars).




3.3 Identifying Urban Hot Spots

Urban Fabric Analysis: Explore how different surface materials, such as concrete and grass, absorb and release heat differently.
Heat Spot Identification: Identify areas where dense built-up land shows noticeably higher temperatures than surrounding areas.




Figure 4: Urban Hot Spots

title: Week 2: Sensor Presentation
text:
4. Personal Reflection

4.1 Earth Observation and Open Data

Data Accessibility: The open-access Landsat archive has made remote sensing data much easier to use for research and long-term environmental monitoring.
Data Fusion Potential: Combining Landsat’s thermal information with Sentinel-2’s finer spatial detail could support more detailed urban microclimate analysis.



4.2 Technical Evolution and Workflow

Xaringan Learning: Learned how to build presentations using Markdown and CSS, which was challenging at first but useful in practice.
Quarto Integration: Began moving toward a Quarto-based workflow for managing documents and portfolio work more consistently (Kirenz 2023).
Reproducibility Mastery: Gained a better understanding of how code and written explanation can be combined in scientific communication (Xie 2022).
Future Research: I hope to apply these reproducible methods in future academic work so that my analysis remains clear and verifiable (Quarto 2022).







Best Practice: Project Separation



As discussed in the module, it is generally better not to carry out complex analysis and portfolio writing in the same R project, because keeping them separate helps maintain clarity and organisation.





Figure 5: Screenshot of the RStudio workspace used for portfolio writing, showing the active document and the project file structure.

title: Week 1: Introduction to Remote Sensing and Spectral Analysis Practice
text:
This week’s learning made me realize that remote sensing is not simply photography, but rather the physical measurement of electromagnetic radiation (EMR) energy interactions (Brady 2021). Through the lecture, I grasped the core formula of electromagnetic waves: \(\lambda = c/v\), where wavelength is inversely proportional to frequency.






Note



Before the sensor detects the Earth’s surface, the energy undergoes complex interactions (Tempfli et al. 2009):

Atmospheric Interactions: These include Rayleigh scattering (which makes the sky blue and affects visible bands), Mie scattering (caused by aerosols), and non-selective scattering (caused by clouds). This explains why optical remote sensing faces massive atmospheric correction challenges in cloudy Guiyang.
Surface Interactions: Energy is absorbed, transmitted, or reflected by the surface. The sensor’s task is precisely to capture this reflected energy, which carries information about the surface features.






The lecture emphasized the “four resolutions” that determine the success of remote sensing applications:

Spatial Resolution: Determines the smallest observable feature. Sentinel-2’s 10m resolution offers a significant advantage at the urban scale (Jensen 2015).
Spectral Resolution: The number and width of bands. I realized that a “spectral signature” is the physical fingerprint of a surface feature, allowing us to distinguish extremely similar materials through their responses across different bands.
Temporal Resolution: The revisit period, which is crucial for dynamic environmental monitoring.
Radiometric Resolution: The sensor’s sensitivity, or quantization level (e.g., 12-bit provides 4,096 brightness levels).




I selected my hometown, Guiyang, as the study area. Beyond my emotional connection, Guiyang holds significant research value geographically:

Complexity of Karst Topography: Guiyang’s rugged terrain leads to a pronounced “Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function (BRDF)” effect, meaning observation and illumination angles significantly alter the apparent reflectance of surface features.
Data Balancing Act: While Sentinel-2 excels in spatial precision, Landsat’s historical data archive remains irreplaceable for tracking the urban expansion trajectory of Guiyang over the past few decades that I recall from my childhood (Tempfli et al. 2009).




Figure 1: Pre-processing workflow in SNAP, including resampling and masking Sentinel-2 data for Guiyang.

title: Week 2: Sensor Presentation
text:
Xaringan mastery: Developed a technical presentation using the xaringan R package to integrate live code with stylistic slides.
Reproducibility: Emphasized a code-driven approach that ensures the seamless transition from data analysis to academic presentation ((Xie, Allaire, and Grolemund 2022)).





Host platform: Access the full interactive presentation hosted via GitHub Pages for the best user experience.
On-page viewing: Utilize the embedded frame below to navigate through the Landsat sensor introduction.
Full-screen mode: Click the provided link in the note below to open the deck in a separate browser tab.







Slide Deck Access



Below is my embedded slide deck regarding the Landsat program. You can interact with the slides directly or click here for the full-screen version.

title: Week 3 Learning Diary: Remote Sensing Data Corrections and Enhancements
text:
This week changed the way I think about satellite imagery. I used to see it as something similar to a digital photo, but now I understand that it is actually a measurement of electromagnetic radiation. Because of that, the original Digital Numbers (DN) cannot be used directly without some form of correction.

Radiometric Calibration: This is the first step in turning raw DN values into physically meaningful spectral radiance.







Core Concept: Radiance vs. Reflectance



Radiance is the total energy coming from the ground to the sensor, while reflectance describes how much incoming light is reflected by the surface. Reflectance is more useful for environmental comparison because it is more consistent across time and space (Joyce 2013).






Geometric Distortions: These can happen because of the sensor angle or terrain effects, and they are usually corrected with Ground Control Points (GCPs).
Atmospheric Correction: The atmosphere can scatter and absorb light, which affects the image. Dark Object Subtraction (DOS) is a traditional way to reduce this effect by assuming the darkest pixels should have close to zero reflectance (Jensen 2015).




In mountainous areas, slope and aspect can change the amount of sunlight reaching the surface, which affects reflectance values. The Cosine Correction Model is one way to reduce this terrain effect by using the incidence angle: \[\cos i = \cos \theta_0 \cos \theta_s + \sin \theta_0 \sin \theta_s \cos(\Phi_0 - \Phi_s)\]

title: Week 3 Learning Diary: Remote Sensing Data Corrections and Enhancements
text:
This week changed the way I think about satellite imagery. I used to see it as something similar to a digital photo, but now I understand that it is actually a measurement of electromagnetic radiation. Because of that, the original Digital Numbers (DN) cannot be used directly without some form of correction.

Radiometric Calibration: This is the first step in turning raw DN values into physically meaningful spectral radiance.







Core Concept: Radiance vs. Reflectance



Radiance is the total energy coming from the ground to the sensor, while reflectance describes how much incoming light is reflected by the surface. Reflectance is more useful for environmental comparison because it is more consistent across time and space (Joyce 2013).






Geometric Distortions: These can happen because of the sensor angle or terrain effects, and they are usually corrected with Ground Control Points (GCPs).
Atmospheric Correction: The atmosphere can scatter and absorb light, which affects the image. Dark Object Subtraction (DOS) is a traditional way to reduce this effect by assuming the darkest pixels should have close to zero reflectance (Jensen 2015).




In mountainous areas, slope and aspect can change the amount of sunlight reaching the surface, which affects reflectance values. The Cosine Correction Model is one way to reduce this terrain effect by using the incidence angle: \[\cos i = \cos \theta_0 \cos \theta_s + \sin \theta_0 \sin \theta_s \cos(\Phi_0 - \Phi_s)\]

title: Week 3 Learning Diary: Remote Sensing Data Corrections and Enhancements
text:
2. Applications of the Content

2.1 Data Acquisition and Methodology
Although this week’s lectures covered radiometric, atmospheric, and topographic corrections in detail, the practical work in this report is mainly about mosaicking, feature extraction, and dimensionality reduction.
For this case study, I used Landsat Collection 2 Level-2 products for the Greater Beijing area, acquired on Jan 31 and Feb 1, 2026.






Why bypass DOS Atmospheric Correction?



Since these Landsat scenes are provided as Analysis Ready Data (ARD), they have already been rigorously atmospherically corrected by the USGS. Applying an extra DOS correction here would redundantly alter the data and introduce errors. Instead, I focused on rescaling the stored integer values into surface reflectance values.





Figure 1: USGS Earth Explorer search interface for Beijing tiles.




2.2 Mosaicking and Rescaling Workflow
Because Landsat data is collected in separate tiles, I first needed to merge the two neighbouring scenes. I used the terra::mosaic() function, and the overlap was averaged with the mean option. This helped reduce obvious boundaries between the two scenes, although some small differences may still remain because they were taken on different dates.


Code
# Mosaicking and Masking to Beijing Boundary
beijing_mosaic <- terra::mosaic(LC08, LC09, fun="mean")

beijing_clipped <- beijing_mosaic %>%
  terra::crop(., beijing_boundary) %>%
  terra::mask(., beijing_boundary)

# Rescale Level-2 bands to Surface Reflectance (SR)
beijing_sr <- (beijing_clipped * 0.0000275) - 0.2

# Simple value-range cleaning
# A simple cleaning step was used to suppress problematic pixels, 
# although no full pixel-level QA cloud mask was implemented in this workflow.
beijing_sr[beijing_sr < -0.2 | beijing_sr > 1.2] <- NA

# 4. Plot the mosaicked study area as a true colour image
terra::plotRGB(
  beijing_clipped,
  r = 4, g = 3, b = 2,
  stretch = "lin",
  main = "Mosaicked Landsat 8/9 Product - Beijing"
)





Figure 2: The mosaicked Landsat 8/9 product clipped to the Beijing boundary.




2.3 Spectral and Structural Feature Extraction

NDVI: Spectral Interpretation






Dealing with Non-physical Anomalies



Because the rescaling equation includes a negative offset (-0.2), certain dark pixels (like water or shadows) can produce denominators close to zero in the NDVI formula. Filtering the data to the theoretical NDVI range of [-1, 1] is essential to remove mathematical artifacts and restore the true visual contrast.




Code
# Calculate NDVI
ndvi <- (beijing_sr[[5]] - beijing_sr[[4]]) / (beijing_sr[[5]] + beijing_sr[[4]])

# Clean physically implausible extreme values:
# remove all anomalous pixels falling outside the theoretical NDVI range of [-1, 1]
ndvi[ndvi < -1 | ndvi > 1] <- NA

# Define a new colour palette
veg_palette <- colorRampPalette(c("#e5e5e5", "#f7fcb9", "#addd8e", "#31a354"))(100)

# Plot the NDVI map again, and fix the display range to improve visual contrast
plot(ndvi, 
     col = veg_palette, 
     zlim = c(-0.2, 0.6),  # fix the display range to enhance visual contrast
     main = "NDVI Analysis - Beijing (Feb 2026)", 
     axes = TRUE)


Interpretation of Figure 3: The NDVI map shows generally low vegetation activity across Beijing in early February, which is consistent with winter dormancy. Slightly higher values are visible in some mountain and vegetated areas. Some white gaps remain after cleaning, indicating pixels with invalid or unstable NDVI values that may be linked to residual cloud contamination, very bright surfaces, water, or other problematic pixels. This highlights the importance of quality control before interpretation.


GLCM Texture: Structural Interpretation


Code
# Compute GLCM Homogeneity using Band 4
library(GLCMTextures)
textures1 <- glcm_textures(
  beijing_sr[[4]],
  w = c(7, 7),
  n_levels = 4,
  quant_method = "range",
  metrics = "glcm_homogeneity"
)

# Plot GLCM Homogeneity
plot(
  textures1,
  main = "GLCM Homogeneity of Beijing",
  col = gray.colors(100)
)


Interpretation of Figure 4: The GLCM homogeneity surface ranges from about 0.65 to 1.00 and appears fairly smooth overall. Lower homogeneity is more visible in parts of the urban plain, where dense built-up structures create greater local variation. By contrast, many northern and peripheral areas appear more homogeneous, although this pattern should be interpreted carefully because the 7x7 window may smooth fine-scale differences and mountain terrain can also introduce local texture complexity.






Figure 3: NDVI Analysis of Beijing.







Figure 4: GLCM Homogeneity map of Beijing.








2.4 Dimensionality Reduction via PCA
Fusing spectral data with spatial metrics increases dataset dimensionality, making Principal Component Analysis (PCA) a highly effective tool to compress the combined multi-band stack into uncorrelated components without losing critical environmental information (Schulte to Bühne 2018).


Code
# Data Fusion: Concatenate the multi-spectral bands and texture features
raster_and_texture <- c(beijing_sr, textures1)

# PCA applied to the combined feature stack (standardized)
pca <- prcomp(
  as.data.frame(raster_and_texture, na.rm = TRUE),
  center = TRUE,
  scale = TRUE
)

# Show PCA summary
summary(pca)

# Project PCA results back to raster space
pca_raster <- predict(raster_and_texture, pca)

# Plot PC1
plot(
  pca_raster[[1]],
  main = "Principal Component 1 (PC1) - Beijing"
)


Interpretation of Figure 5 & 6: The summary table shows that PC1 explains approximately 76% of the standardized variance. The spatial projection of PC1 suggests that, in addition to broad landscape differences, some of the highest values are likely associated with residual clouds or haze that remain visible in the true-colour composite. Because PCA captures the strongest sources of variance, it is sensitive to extreme reflectance values. This suggests that incomplete cloud masking may have influenced the first component and therefore affected downstream interpretation.



Figure 5: PCA Summary table showing cumulative proportion of variance.





Figure 6: Spatial projection of Principal Component 1 (PC1) over Beijing.

title: Week 3 Learning Diary: Remote Sensing Data Corrections and Enhancements
text:
3. Personal Reflection

3.1 Understanding the “Black Box”
Even though modern platforms now provide Analysis Ready Data (ARD), I think it is still important to understand what is happening behind the scenes. If I work with raw drone imagery or data from less processed sensors in the future, I will need to understand methods like DOS and radiometric calibration in order to make sure my results are reliable.


3.2 Methodological Limitations and Future Improvements
This week’s exercise made me realise how important pre-processing is for later analysis. There are two main things I would improve next time:

QA-based Cloud Masking: In this workflow, I mainly relied on visual checking and simple value-range filtering, which means some remaining cloud or shadow contamination may still be present. In future work, I would like to use the QA_PIXEL band to mask clouds and shadows more properly.
Topographic Correction: This would be especially useful for northern Beijing because of the mountainous terrain. I did not include it this time because of time and data limitations, but it would be a valuable next step if I wanted more reliable results in mountain areas.

title: Week 3 Learning Diary: Remote Sensing Data Corrections and Enhancements
text:
4. References


Jensen, John R. 2015. Introductory Digital Image Processing: A Remote Sensing Perspective. 4th ed. Pearson. https://books.google.com/books/about/Introductory_Digital_Image_Processing.html?id=BWx3CgAAQBAJ.


Joyce, Karen. 2013. “Radiative Transfer and Atmospheric Correction Video.” 2013. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qb4yFwzsnU8&t.


Schulte to Bühne, Pettorelli, H. 2018. “Better Together: Integrating and Fusing Multispectral and Radar Satellite Imagery to Inform Biodiversity Monitoring, Ecological Research and Conservation Science.” Methods in Ecology and Evolution 9 (4): 849–65. https://doi.org/10.1111/2041-210X.12942.
